methods.description: Process for assembling and configuring the 1D diatomic granular crystal chains and associated equipment (actuator, force sensor, static load mechanism) for wave propagation experiments.

steps.description: Assemble five different 1D diatomic granular crystals composed of three-particle, sphere-cylinder-sphere, repeating unit cells as shown in Fig. 1(a).
steps.searchable_summary: assemble 1D diatomic granular crystals with specified unit cell structure
outputs: five different assembled granular crystal chains

steps.description: The particles (spheres and cylinders) are made from 440C stainless steel, with radius R = 9.53 mm, elastic modulus E = 200 GPa, and Poisson's ratio ν = 0.3.
steps.searchable_summary: specify particle material and properties

steps.description: Each of the five chains is assembled with cylinders of a different length, L = [9.4, 12.5, 15.8, 18.7, 21.9] mm.
steps.searchable_summary: specify cylinder lengths for each chain type
outputs: cylinder length specifications for each chain

steps.description: The chains are 21 particles (7 unit cells) long.
steps.searchable_summary: specify total chain length

steps.description: The mass of the spherical particles is measured to be m = 27.8 g and the mass of the cylindrical particles is measured to be M = [20.5, 27.3, 34.1, 40.7, 47.8] g for each of the corresponding cylinder lengths.
steps.searchable_summary: measure particle masses

steps.description: We align the spheres and cylinders in a horizontal 1D configuration using a containment structure of four polycarbonate rods (12.7 mm diameter). We hold the polycarbonate rods in place with polycarbonate guide plates spaced at intervals of 1 unit cell.
steps.searchable_summary: align particles horizontally using containment structure
outputs: horizontally aligned granular chains within containment

steps.description: We apply low amplitude broadband noise to the granular crystals using a piezoelectric actuator mounted on a steel cube of height 88.9 mm which is fixed to the table.
steps.searchable_summary: mount piezoelectric actuator for excitation

steps.description: The force sensor is composed of a piezo-electric disk embedded with epoxy inside two halves of a R = 9.53 mm, 316 stainless steel sphere... The assembled force sensor is calibrated against a commercial dynamic force sensor, and has a measured total mass and resonant frequency of 28.0 g and 80 kHz, respectively.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare and calibrate dynamic force sensor

steps.description: We insert the dynamic force sensor in place of the last particle, located at the opposite end of the crystal from the actuator.
steps.searchable_summary: insert dynamic force sensor into the granular chain
outputs: granular chain with sensor at the end

steps.description: We condition its output with a 30 kHz cutoff 8-pole butterworth low pass filter and voltage amplifier.
steps.searchable_summary: condition force sensor output signal
inputs: low pass filter (30 kHz cutoff, 8-pole butterworth)

steps.description: At the opposite end of the crystal with respect to the piezoelectric actuator, we apply a static compressive force, F 0 , using a soft (compared to the contact stiffness of the particles) stainless steel linear compression spring (stiffness 1.24 kN/m).
steps.searchable_summary: apply static compressive force using a spring

steps.description: The static compressive force applied to the chain is adjusted by positioning, and fixing to the table, a movable steel cube of height 76.2 mm so that the soft linear spring is compresssed.
steps.searchable_summary: adjust static force by positioning a steel cube

steps.description: The resulting applied static load is measured with a static load cell placed in between the steel cube and the spring.
steps.searchable_summary: measure applied static load

methods.description: Procedure for exciting the granular crystal system with noise and measuring the dynamic force response to characterize the linear spectrum and transfer function, and comparing results to theoretical predictions.

steps.description: We experimentally characterize the linear spectrum of the previously described diatomic chains with sphere-cylinder-sphere unit cells for varied cylinder length and static load.
steps.searchable_summary: use experimental setup to characterize linear spectrum
outputs: experimental data for linear spectrum characterization

steps.description: We apply a low-amplitude (approximately 200 mN peak) bandwidth limited (3 − 15 kHz) noise excitation with the piezoelectric actuator.
steps.searchable_summary: apply noise excitation to granular chain

steps.description: We measure the dynamic force using a sensor embedded in the last particle of the granular crystal as shown in Fig. 1 Fig. 4.
steps.searchable_summary: measure dynamic force response

steps.description: We plot the experimentally determined PSD transfer functions for the five previously described diatomic (three-particle unit cell) chains with varied cylinder length for fixed F 0 =20 N static compression (Fig. 5(a)), and static compression F 0 = [20,25,30,35,40] N, for fixed cylinder length L = 12.5 mm (M = 27.3 g) (Fig. 5(b)).
steps.searchable_summary: calculate and plot experimental PSD transfer functions

steps.description: We plot with solid white lines the cutoff frequencies from the dispersion relation calculated using the experimentally determined Hertz contact coefficients A 1,exp and A 2,exp .
steps.searchable_summary: calculate theoretical cutoff frequencies using experimental coefficients
inputs: experimentally determined Hertz contact coefficients (A1,exp, A2,exp)

steps.description: We plot with solid white lines the cutoff frequencies from the dispersion relation calculated using the experimentally determined Hertz contact coefficients A 1,exp and A 2,exp .
steps.searchable_summary: overlay calculated cutoff frequencies on experimental PSD plots
outputs: plots showing experimental spectra and theoretical cutoffs

steps.description: We compare the experimentally determined A 1,exp and A 2,exp to the theoretically determined A 1 and A 2 in Table I...
steps.searchable_summary: compare experimental and theoretical contact coefficients

steps.description: By comparing Fig. 5 to Fig. 3 we observe good qualitiative agreement between the numerical (state-space) and experimental spectra.
steps.searchable_summary: qualitatively compare experimental and numerical spectra

methods.description: Theoretical modeling of the finite granular crystal system using a state-space approach to compute the frequency response (bode diagram) for comparison with experimental results.

steps.description: We study the finite linearized system corresponding to our experimental setup as shown in Fig. 1(b).
steps.searchable_summary: model the finite linearized system corresponding to the experimental setup
inputs: experimental setup parameters (particle properties, dimensions, chain length)

steps.description: We model the actuator boundary of our system as a fixed 440C steel wall. We model the other end of the chain as a free boundary, as the stiffness of the spring used for static compression is much less than the characteristic stiffness of the particles in contact.
steps.searchable_summary: define boundary conditions for the system model
outputs: system model with defined boundary conditions (fixed, free)

steps.description: The linearized equations of motion for the finite system are the same as Eqs. (4), except the equations for the first and last particles which are given by the following expressions: ...
steps.searchable_summary: use linearized equations of motion for the finite system
outputs: linearized equations of motion for the specific finite system

steps.description: Next, we apply the state-space approach, which can be written as 34 : where vectors x, u, and y are the state, input, and output vectors, respectively. Matrices A, B, C, and D are called state, input, output and direct transmission matrices, respectively.
steps.searchable_summary: apply the state-space approach to the system equations
outputs: state-space representation structure (vectors x, u, y; matrices A, B, C, D)

steps.description: We choose as an input to the system the force F 1 , i.e., u = F 1 ...
steps.searchable_summary: define the system input vector

steps.description: ...and as an output , the averaged force of the two contacts of the last particle (which is analogous to what is measured by the embedded dynamic force sensor in our experimental setup) 12,24,25,33 .
steps.searchable_summary: define the system output vector

steps.description: Thus, for the linear system of Fig. 1(b), we obtain . . 0 , and D = 0. 0 is a zero matrix and I is the identity matrix (both of size N × N).
steps.searchable_summary: derive state-space matrices (A, B, C, D)

steps.description: We use the formulation in Eqs. 9 with MATLAB's (R2008b) bode function to compute the bode diagram of the frequency response for the experimental configurations described.
steps.searchable_summary: compute bode diagram (frequency response) using MATLAB
outputs: bode diagram data (frequency response magnitude and phase)
